extracted.Students: 4151 learners in ASSISTments2009, 19840 learners in ASSISTments2015, 333 learners in Statics2011 and 4000 synthetic learners
extracted.Courses: Each dataset refers to its own context
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Current KT models consider the whole sequence of answers and not specifically the most recent answers. 

All methods used the embedding of answers directly into the model.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors introduce convolution to identify local interactions and patterns in the sequence of answers:

The vector is converted into a matrix.

CNNs are used to reduce dimensionality and locally encode potential patterns.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Sequence vectors are reshaped into a Matrix, which is given as argument to a Convolutional Layer, followed by an average pooling layer.

These are then given as iput to a LSTM.
extracted.Comments:
Authors develop a new apporach at knowledge tracing. Models are followed by a set of optimization attempts. 

Authors argue that this KT approach can help professors and learners track the learning progress of learners and check exercise realtionships.
extracted.Limitations:
Model evaluation should use ranking instead of only showing performance results.

More metrics of evaluation should be considered.

annotations.text: KT looks at past responses from students and uses them to create a model of the students’ knowledge. This model can later be used to predict the probability of getting a new question right. Good KT models can help students identify where a student is lacking in terms of knowledge, which can also be used by professors to better prepare new materials.

Deep learning models in KT seem to be the state-of-the-art but seem to underestimate the contribution of the most recent answers for the current knowledge state, which is counterintuitive. To address this, the authors propose a Convolutional KT model, that uses 3D convolutions.
CKT is argued to achieve the new State-of-te-art performance in predicting performance on ASSISTments2009 and ASSISTments 2015 datasets.
